children.title: Visualizing High-Dimensional Data in Fashion
children.summary: The discussion covers the visualization of high-dimensional data, specifically using plots to differentiate between conservative, neutral, and liberal bills based on principal components. A transition is made to another example involving high-dimensional data from pictures, where each pixel represents a feature, focusing on black and white images. The segment also includes a demonstration of data visualization techniques using scatter plots to represent voting data, with attributes such as party affiliation and gender.

We'd expect these bills are conservative bills. These are, you know, neutral bills and these are very liberal bills. Can see that from this plot. Another way to visualize the data, understand what the principal components are telling us. All right, any questions from what we've done up to this point? Here goes one more demo with fashion. Questions? Questions online? Nothing big, okay. Let's do fashion. Super fun. So, another source of high-dimensional data you encounter is pictures. Right, each pixel is a feature. children.title: Introduction to Fashion MNIST Dataset and PCA
children.summary: The Fashion MNIST dataset consists of images labeled with clothing items like dresses, coats, t-shirts, and pullovers. It includes 60,000 training images and 10,000 test images, each 28x28 pixels in black and white. The focus is on understanding the structure of the data rather than classification. The class names are provided, and a dictionary is created to map class indices to names. The segment encourages experimentation with the dataset and mentions the use of PCA (Principal Component Analysis) for further analysis.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: This is a famous dataset for machine learning people. We have an image in a label and a corresponds to that picture of a dress, a coat, a t-shirt, a pullover. Here's the things we got. These are our pictures. They have labels, this label data, we can go off into classification. We're not going to do that. We want to understand what is the structure. Does it pull over? Is it more like a shirt or a plow? T-shirt. You probably know what that is, but maybe I didn't. Maybe I don't understand Tasha. I understand how this space looks. Where are these points lit? and look at each class. I post this code so you can play with the code and try different samples of it. Alright, let's do PCA. So we have... 5,000 by 28 by 28. My images, so 5,000 images, they're 28 pixels by 28 pixels. Black and white so just how bright is that pixel. Video: 1: File

Visualizing images
The following snippet of code visualizes the images
[ ]: def show_images (images, ncols=5, max_images=30):
# conver the subset of images into a n, 28, 28 matrix for facet visualization
img_mat = np. array(images. head(max_images) ['images ' ] .to_list ( ) )
fig = px. imshow (img_mat, color_continuous_scale='gray',
facet_col = 0, facet_col_wrap=ncols,
height = 220*int(np.ceil(len(images)/ncols)))
fig. update_layout (coloraxis_showscale=False)
# Extract the facet number and convert it back to the class label.
fig. for_each_annotation(lambda a: a. update(text=images. iloc[int(a. text. split("=") [-1] ) ] ['class' ] ) )
return fig
show images ( images. head (20) )

children.title: PCA Implementation for Image Data
children.summary: The discussion focuses on reshaping image data into a 784-dimensional vector by unrolling 28x28 pixel images. The mean is then subtracted to center the data. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is applied using SKLearn with 50 components to analyze the variance in the dataset. It is noted that the data likely exists in a 10-dimensional space due to the presence of 10 clothing classes, with the argument that similar types (e.g., t-shirts and pullovers) may reduce dimensionality further.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Re-shape that into 5,784. D is big, 784. Strike off the mean. standardizing again because these pixels are in the rough pixel magnifus space. I don't need to standardize. What units are about the same? I'm gonna run PCI and take the first 50 principal components. I'll probably just use one or two, maybe three, but I had 15 in case I wanted to look more. I get the explain variance ratios that's built into the PCA object save some time here We'll spy by 100. percentage. We can see here there's a little more structure. Here's some data in first, second, third, fourth, fifth, sixth, seventh, all we have to 10. Interesting. It goes to about 10. Why would it go to about 10? Important detail, there are 10 classes, 10 types of clothing here. So in some sense, this data really lives in something close to a 10-dimensional space, but I'd argue t-shirts and pullovers are pretty similar. Ankle boots and heels are probably pretty similar, so it kind of decays quickly. Video: 1: File

children.title: PCA Visualization and Upcoming Lectures
children.summary: The segment covers the visualization of clothing data using Principal Component Analysis (PCA), focusing on the extraction of the first three principal components and the relationships between different clothing types in the PCA space. It emphasizes that most of the data can be explained within the first two or three dimensions, indicating a clear structure. The discussion also highlights PCA's role in visualizing high-dimensional data and its limitations for linear modeling. Additionally, the upcoming lecture schedule is outlined, with a focus on clustering algorithms and neural networks, including a detailed look at chatbots like ChatGPT, along with Thanksgiving wishes.

children.title: PCA Visualization of Clothing Data
children.summary: The segment discusses the visualization of clothing data using Principal Component Analysis (PCA). It highlights the extraction of the first three principal components and visualizes the first two dimensions to reveal structures within the data. Different clothing types, such as trousers, coats, dresses, and various shoes, are plotted to show their relationships in the PCA space. The analysis indicates that most of the data can be explained within the first two or three dimensions, suggesting a clear structure in the dataset.

children.title: PCA for Data Visualization and Modeling
children.summary: PCA is utilized for visualizing high-dimensional data by projecting it onto lower dimensions, which can help in identifying clusters within the data, such as groups of jeans in a plot. While PCA simplifies data for visualization, it may not be suitable for linear models due to the nature of data projection already involved in these models. An additional matrix multiplication through PCA may not yield significant benefits, and students are encouraged to consider this as an exercise. The lecture concludes with a note about the upcoming schedule, mentioning one lecture before Thanksgiving break.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I can start to see hey these jeans seem to cluster in how they are expressed in different parts of my plot. Now I might want to study these groups of jeans together. Right. All right, so this is how we use PCA for for data visualization. It is how we take high-dimensional data like images, votes and try to make sense of them in lower dimensions for visualization. And again, for some modeling techniques you might do this too, you might apply PCA to simplify your data. For linear models, it would not make sense to do that. I don't have enough time to explain why, but maybe you can think about it. So remember PCA is going to just project my data onto a matrix W and just some onto my principal components. And all my linear models are also projecting my data onto matrix. So Doing an additional matrix multiply may not do much. You can think about why that is. Leave that for an exercise. Alright. That is it for PCA. Starting next week, we have just one lecture and then we have Thanksgiving break. Video: 1: File

children.title: Upcoming Lectures on Clustering and Neural Networks
children.summary: Next week's lecture will focus on clustering, an algorithm for grouping data. Following that, the lecture will introduce neural networks and large language models, including a detailed explanation of how chatbots like ChatGPT function. The last two lectures are expected to be engaging, and the instructor wishes everyone a happy Thanksgiving and looks forward to seeing students in the upcoming sessions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Next week's lecture is on clustering, which is another really fun algorithm for how we break our data into different groups. And then you get a break and we come back and you should all come to lecture. We're going to do neural nets for the first time and then we'll do large language models and I will explain how a chat to BT works in all of its detail. And so after that lecture, it will no longer be magic. So the last two lectures should be a lot of fun. Have a great Thanksgiving. I look forward to seeing a few of you on Tuesday and then hopefully the rest of you. The week after. Bye. Video: 1: File

leaf_chunk.audio: Next week's lecture is on clustering, which is another really fun algorithm for how we break our data into different groups. And then you get a break and we come back and you should all come to lecture. We're going to do neural nets for the first time and then we'll do large language models and I will explain how a chat to BT works in all of its detail. And so after that lecture, it will no longer be magic. So the last two lectures should be a lot of fun. Have a great Thanksgiving. I look forward to seeing a few of you on Tuesday and then hopefully the rest of you. The week after. Bye.
